question: Why does the earth 's rotation affect a pendulum , but not anything else that hovers above the ground ? If the earth is truly rotating , atmosphere and all , then the Foucault pendulum would n't be possible whatsoever , would it ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["The * Coriolis effect * happens when you 're on a rotating surface and you change your distance from the center of rotation . It 's easiest to explain if you think about standing on a merry - go - round . All the horses on the merry - go - round make one full rotation in the same amount of time . But the horses farther from the center have a longer distance to travel in that same amount of time . So they 're moving faster than the horses near the center . What if you try to jump from one horse to another ? If you 're on a horse near the center and you jump outwards , you 're jumping from a slower horse to a faster horse . As you jump outwards , you 're moving with the same speed as the slower horse was ( because of inertia ) , but suddenly you 're in a zone of faster horses . The horse you were trying to jump onto is moving faster than you are , so it outpaces you and you fall on the floor behind it . In other words , moving outwards from the center of rotation causes you to drift backwards , from the point of view of the rotating merry - go - round . The opposite happens if you jump from one of the outer horses inwards - you 're moving faster than the horse you 're aiming at , so you drift forwards . The same thing happens on Earth 's surface . The North and South Poles are at the axis of rotation , so they have no lateral speed . But the Equator is moving at over 1000 mph . So as you move from one of the Poles toward the Equator , you will drift \" backwards \" with respect to Earth 's rotation ( i.e. westward ) . If you 're moving from the Equator toward one of the Poles , you 'll drift \" forwards \" ( eastward ) . This affects any floating object to some extent . But it 's a very small effect - you only see it with things that are really really big ( like ocean currents ) or really really fast ( like long - range artillery ) .", "Well , it does . It is known as the coriolis effect . The Foucault pendulum is just a way to show that the earth is rotating by being able to measure the changes in the path of the pendulum as the earth rotates .", "It 's not so much that the earth 's rotation has an effect on the pendulum , it 's actually that it * does n't * . What 's really happening is that the pendulum keeps moving in the same direction , but the earth is rotating underneath it . There is an animation [ here ] ( URL_0 ) that shows what 's really going on ."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Earth’s spin does influence everything sitting on or near its surface, including things floating above the ground. But most of the time, that influence is tiny, so you don’t really notice it.

Take a ball dropped from a height: gravity pulls it straight down, and Earth’s rotation barely changes its path. The ball is small, and air resistance doesn’t make the rotation effect stand out much.

A pendulum is different. It’s a long, heavy object that keeps swinging back and forth, and its motion is slowed a little by air friction and by interaction with the ground. Because it swings for a while, the fact that the ground beneath it is moving with Earth’s rotation becomes noticeable.

So the pendulum seems to shift direction over time because it’s moving relative to the ground, and the ground itself is rotating. That’s why Earth’s rotation shows up clearly in a Foucault pendulum, even though the same rotation is much harder to spot in most other hovering objects."]

question: Why are propellers on the back of boats , but the front of airplanes ? Never made sense to me . Seems like airplanes play by their own set of rules Please explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["For reasons of balance , it 's much easier to have the engine at the front of a plane than at the back - unless you 're flying a canard , which has the wings at the back and the \" tailplane \" at the front . Boats , on the other hand , have a lot less problems with balance , and it 's easier to put the engine amidships or towards the back . Add to that water being more viscous so you do n't want high - speed flow dragging all along the hull , it makes sense to put the prop at the back .", "Lots of reasons- If you put the propeller at the back of the plane- then when you rotate on takeoff or flare for landing- you put the propeller dangerously close to the ground . Airflow over the fuselage and wings gets pretty turbulent and when that air interacts with the propeller the propeller becomes noisier and less efficient . Waterflow over a boat hull tends to be a lot less turbulent and the propellers are either spinning much slower ( large boat ) or located below the hull where there is even less turbulence ( smaller boats ) . Cooling an air cooled engine can be difficult when the fuselage is in the way . Putting the engine in front greatly simplifies cooling baffles and such . The airflow from the propeller flowing over the wings helps generate additional lift you would n't have in a pusher configuration . Weight and balance is an issue . Putting the engine at the back generally means moving a lot of weight aft or using long drive shafts . Control surfaces are simpler and arguably better tested when they 're in the back and the engine would get in the way of putting them there . Edit : Almost forgot the most important reason ! Anything kicked up by the tires in a pusher configuration is going to pass right through the propeller and nick and otherwise damage it . This is a serious issue on unimproved airstrips- but even on a paved runway you kick up debris . Look at what happened to the Concorde when it kicked up a small metal wear strip . This is n't a factor for a boat . There are notable exceptions that do use a pusher configuration- The rear engine on a [ Cessna Skymaster ] ( URL_0 ) is a pusher ( and that engine has an overheating problem ) . Many small seaplanes are pushers with the engine mounted up high in a pod on the wing or on a pylon . The [ Beech Starship ] ( URL_2 ) was a twin engine pusher canard . It was the first real production composite airframe and the first business aircraft to have an all glass cockpit . The [ Piaggio P180 Avanti ] ( URL_1 ) is another twin engine pusher aircraft with a canard- but it also has a convention T tail as well . The P180 makes a very distinctive sound due to the interference between the propellers and the airflow over the wings and from the exuast .", "The shape of the boat is why . ( Though , some airplanes in \" pusher \" configuratiin have had their prop(s ) in back . ) With a boat , it is easier to install a prop in back , with a shorter driveshaft while keeping the center of gravity more rearward . You do n't want the front of your boat to be lower than the back . Boats generally move faster with the bow angled up ."]
chatgpt_answers:
["The reason propellers sit at the back of boats but the front of airplanes has to do with how each one moves through its environment.

Boats travel through water, and water is thick and resistant compared with air. As the boat moves, the hull pushes through that water, and the propeller at the rear spins to shove water backward. That backward push makes the boat go forward.

Airplanes are different because they move through air, which is much thinner than water. When an airplane is moving, its wings create lift, a force that acts upward and helps the plane stay in the sky. The wings are shaped specifically to make lift as air flows over them. To make the plane go forward, the engine drives a propeller mounted in front, and that propeller pulls the airplane through the air.

So, in simple terms: boats use a rear propeller to push against water, while airplanes use a front propeller to pull through air."]

question: What are Arrays , Vectors , and Matrices I just started learning MatLab and I keep hearing these terms used but I do n't know what they are or how they relate to eachother . Please explain like I'm five.

question: What s the purpose of logarithmic scales like Fujita , Richter , and Decibel ? why not use linear scales ? They seem un - intuitive and illogical . Please explain like I'm five.

And then we have to say that the 2011 earthquake in Japan ( a 9.0 ) is a 100,000,000 . Just stupidly large numbers . That 's the illogical thing . Not everything follows a useful linear pattern . The numbers never get to the levels which require scientific notation . 60db=1,000,000/1"]
chatgpt_answers:
["Logarithmic scales are handy when you need to measure things that can swing across an enormous range. The Richter scale measures earthquake strength, the Fujita scale measures tornado intensity, and the Decibel scale measures how loud sound is.

Linear scales are better for things that stay within a fairly small range. A ruler is a good example of a linear scale, since it measures length in a straightforward way.

The reason logarithmic scales are so useful is that they squeeze a huge spread of values into a much smaller scale. They do this by using a logarithmic function to compress the numbers so they fit more neatly.

If earthquakes were measured on a linear scale, the scale would have to be incredibly long to cover every possible strength. With a logarithmic scale like the Richter scale, the same range can be shown in a much shorter span.

And in some cases, logarithmic scales actually match how we experience things. Our ears don’t respond to sound in a simple linear way; they are more sensitive to changes over certain ranges. That’s why the Decibel scale is logarithmic.

So the short version is: logarithmic scales are useful for measuring things that vary a lot, and sometimes they fit human senses better than linear scales do."]

question: How do tattoo last so long if every cell in your body is replaced in 7 years ? I am aware that they fade , has that go to do with cells recycling ? Please explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Tattoos last forever because the human body thinks it is under attack when someone draws on it . The body ’s complex processes that keep our skin free from infection are the same ones that allow ink to live forever in our skin . It should very difficult for anything to stay in the skin : humans shed about 40,000 skin cells per hour , or around 1,000,000 per day . Anything drawn onto the skin would gradually flake or get washed off . But tattoos are done by using machines to punch through the outer layer of skin and into the dermis , which is the deeper layer of skin that does n’t flake off . They do that by pushing small needles over and over , quickly , pushing the ink deep into the fibers of the dermis . Pushing so far into the skin is essentially a wound , and the body reacts as you ’d expect it to . The immune system reacts to what it considers to be an attack by sending cells to the wound and start repairing the skin — that process might sound traumatic , but it ’s actually what allows tattoos to stick around for so long . The cells , known as macrophages , travel to the site of the wound and start eating up the ink that has been deposited there . Other cells and parts of the skin , such as fibroblasts , suck up the tattoo ink . But they ca n’t do anything with it , and so stay stuck in that layer , visible through the skin . Some of the ink will go into the upper layer of the skin , and that will then be shed as normal . That ’s why the process of getting a tattoo also includes time letting your skin heal , with the body taking two to four weeks to initially heal . The body also keeps fighting the foreign ink throughout its owners ’ life . That means that tattoos will gradually fade over time , a process that is also sped up by other things like light from the sun .", "As an aside , it 's just not true that every cell in our bodies is replaced over seven years : URL_0", "Tattoos are n't made up of your cells . It is ink injected into the lower layers of your skin . Your skin cells replace themselves and die off constantly . This is happening * around * the ink but does n't affect the ink . Tattoos fade primarily due to Sun exposure . UV light breaks down the ink molecules ."]

question: What happens when a power company generates too much power ? When a power generation station produces too much power what do they do with the excess ? Who pays for , what I 'm assuming , is wasted output ? Please explain like I'm five.

If you connect two generators together , then they will lock their rotation together . So all the rotation of all the generators in a grid are locked together in perfect sync . This means all the generators start speeding up . The grid manager will give instructions to all the power plants telling them how much power to produce , but they will also give out instructions telling the power plants what to do if the speed of the generator changes . In the US , power plants aim for 1800 rpm generator speed , but the grid manager might tell some power plants that they should automatically start reducing power if the speed goes over 1802 rpm , and , for example , drop down to 50 % by 1806 rpm . The exact instructions depend on what the power plant is capable of doing , and whether the power plant operator wants some compensation payments for reducing power . The power plant operators will try to sell power in advance to utilities in an auction system . Then , they 'll tell the grid manager , how much power generation they have booked , and whether they are fully booked , and what price their spare capacity is , as well as whether they are willing to reduce power , and how much compensation they they want if they have to reduce power . The job of the grid manager is then to try to work out which power plants give the best value for handling sudden unexpected power shortages or power excesses , and let them handle the fluctuations . Just be glad you do n't have to work out all the billing between these different companies , trying to match what was pre - arranged , with what actually happened , and all the billing for the automatic controls , compensation for reduced output , etc .", "In my simple perception of the world , if the electricity is not used , the generators will speed up , so they need to throttle the steam to the turbine .", "A generator does n't really produce excess power . It produces the amount of power that is required by the load . Let ’s look at a simple definition of a generator . A generator takes mechanical , physical or potential energy , and transforms it into electrical energy . Whether its wind , hydro , or a simple gas gennie , it changes one form of energy into electrical potential . Ok , with that out of the way . Let 's say you have a generator rated for 10 megawatts ( MW ) . That means this generator can output safely , and continuously up to 10MW , and provide power of a range from 0MW(no load ) to 10MW(full load ) . The amount of MW produced is actually load dependent . If your load only requires 5MW , your generator is not going to provide more than 5MW , but you still have the ability to provide up to 10MW . So , there 's really no wasted output per se , but there 's the capability of generating up to 10MW . If number crunching and formulas are your thing . Power = Volts x Amps . If it 's not , just keep in mind , voltage is regulated as it would n't be good for equipment if you had huge fluctuating voltages , so therefore current ( Amps ) is linearly proportional to your load requirements . As load goes up , amps goes up , and therefore the force at which you are required to turn your generator goes up . This goes back to the mechanical energy into electrical energy point I made earlier . If load goes down , you throttle back whatever is running your generator . If it 's hydro , you 're lowering the head gate , decreasing the amount of water pushing your turbine . If it 's a gas gennie , the governor is throttling back your fuel output . ( Wind is different , since you ca n't control the wind . You just end up shutting down units rather than throttling . ) I really try not to look at generator in terms of speed , as that must be kept consistent to keep the 60Hz frequency in North America . But if you look at it in terms of the amount of force required to meet demand , I think it makes more sense . There 's a lot more to power generation than this , and I can go into more detail about that , if you want . And to wrap it all up together , rather than have 1x10MW generator , lets change that to 5x2MW generators , but still have the 5MW load requirements . You can spread the 5MW load across 3x2MW generators and leave the other 2 on standby . A spike in load hopefully wo n't bog down your online generators , and buy you enough time to get the other two online if your load keeps increasing . So there you have it . There 's no real excess power production , just load capability . If you have a lot of generating capability , you can think about selling power outside of your power grid to neighbouring utilities . question: How do " hiveminds " of ant colonies actually work ? Together , ants seem to be capable of highly coordinated efforts between thousands of ants . It 's just amazing to me . How does that coordination actually work ? Please explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["A hive mind is n't a single mind in the way sci fi shows make you think . It would be more accurate to think of it in terms of a great many individuals , each behaving in such a way that would be fairly stupid for that individual ... but when everyone does it , it becomes pretty smart . Let me give you an example . When ants forage for food , they just wander around in a mostly random manner , hoping to stumble across a food source . * Let 's say ant A is foraging for food , wandering randomly until it finds a discarded muffin . * Ant A grabs a muffin crumb and heads back to the colony , at the same time it puts down a scent trail from the muffin to the colony . * Ant B is also foraging randomly but it finds the scent trail laid down by ant A. Ant B has a very simple decision to make . Either follow the scent trail to see if something 's at the source of it or continue to forage randomly . * Let 's say it 's a 50/50 decision for ant B and for the sake of argument , ant B decides to follow the scent trail . * Ant B finds the muffin , grabs a crum , heads back to the nest and lays down his own scent trail , reinforcing the trail laid down by ant A. * Ant C is foraging randomly and also finds the scent trail but by now it 's been reinforced by ant B. The much stronger scent trail makes it more likely that ant C will follow the trail instead of continuing to forage randomly . * Ant C decides to follow the trail , grabs a crumb and heads back to the colony . Also reinforcing the scent trail . * For foraging ant D it 's even more likely that he 'll follow the trail instead of continuing his random search pattern after ant C reinforced the trail . * and so on , and so on . Now each of these ants is behaving in exactly the same way . They all forage randomly until they find either food or a scent trail and then make a decision on whether the continue their random search or to follow the scent trail . The primary variable is that the stronger the scent trail is , the more likely any new ants that find the trail are the follow it . In practical terms this means that a small food source will attract very few ants . The scent trail will be very weak because the food source will be gone before many ants are attracted . Any ants that follow the weak scent trail and find no food , wo n't reinforce the trail and just go back to searching randomly . Without frequent reinforcement , the trail will just dissipate fairly quickly . A large food source on the other hand will keep attracting more and more ants as the scent trail keeps getting stronger and more persuasive to fresh ants that find the trail . Individually all those ants are behaving in the same , not so intelligent way . They 're not even directly communicating to each other , they just have instinctive responses to finding food or scent trails . But the net result is that the ant colony as a whole efficiently assigns the appropriate number of ants to deal with large and small food sources . Large food sources attract many ants . Small food sources will never waste the time of many ants . That 's essentially what a hive mind is . Many individuals displaying simple behaviour that compounds into complex results when many individuals behave that way together .", "Their minds are not actually linked , but they all share similar instincts which have been developed over thousands of years . These instincts tell them how to act as a hive and follow each other . Colonies of animals also use pheromones which are chemical messages that other animals can detect . These can warn the colony of danger , food , etc . The culmination of instinct and pheromones make it look like the entire colony share one mind .", "Not really a hive mind in the sci - fi sense . Each ant just has the genetically coded instinct to perform a certain role , such as protecting from invaders , collecting food , or in the Queen 's case , reproduction . They use pheromone trains to communicate sources of food , danger , etc"]
chatgpt_answers: ["Ants coordinate by using chemicals and very basic actions. Each ant has a job in the colony, and they pass messages with chemical signals called pheromones. For instance, if one ant discovers food, it leaves a pheromone trail behind so others can follow it. When more ants smell that trail, they’re more likely to go the same way, which helps the colony find the food and carry it back.\n\nThey also communicate by touching antennae, and they make decisions with simple rules like following other ants or joining the biggest group. Put together, these small methods let thousands of ants act like a team, even without one boss or any complicated language."]

question: What exactly happens when I click on " Safely Eject " for a USB or external hard drive ? . Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["When you save a file to disk or to a USB stick , it usually is not written completely at the moment of saving . Writing to disk means that your file is written to a part of your computer 's memory , after which the operating system tells your program the file has been saved . In the background , the data of your file is then transferred from this * memory buffer * to the storage medium ( that is : your disk or USB stick ) . So after you have saved a file , parts of it remain in memory and have not yet been written out to the storage medium . However , your program has told * you * that the file was successfully stored . When you prepare a USB stick or external hard disk for ejection , you tell your computer to finish writing out all data that is currently lingering in the memory buffer , while at the same time rejecting any new files to be written to the device . After the buffer has been * flushed * ( that is what it 's called ) all parts of all your file(s ) are actually stored on the hard disk or USB stick and it is safe to disconnect it from your computer . The real - life situation is a bit more complicated , because in addition to writing the file 's content to the storage medium , it also takes some housekeeping reads and writes to register * where * exactly your data was stored in the file system of the external drive or USB stick . tl;dr : pending data needs to be written out to the storage medium from the memory buffer . Edit : removed some * actually*s and polished up lightly .", "No one knows , it has never been done", "The reason this \" safely remove \" option exists is just as a sort of double - check that you 're not removing the drive in the middle of a file transfer . Most external drives now have an activity light that blinks when the drive is in use , and you 're fine just going by that . As long as it 's not blinking , the drive can be removed at any time ."]

question: DNA Microarrays and their uses in a clinical aspect . I 've been reading papers and wiki 's and I can not seem to get my head around the concept . Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Traditionally staining and microscopes have been used to determine properties of tumors and normal cells ( i.e from their histology ) . DNA Microarray techniques are an extension and eventually replacement for doing this , as it potentially identifies the exact mutation(s ) that causes the normal cell to become a ( potentially dangerous ) tumor . Knowing what 's happening on a genetic basis gives the opportunity to better predict a prognosis , and give a more accurate ( hence more meaningful and possibly effective ) therapy . Edit : spelling .", "So this is in the context of cancer research and by a non - PhD ( so I basically understand this LI5 ): If we take a chunk of tissue from a person or animal , we can put that tissue on a DNA Microarray and run it through a very large , very expensive machine and it will tell us what genes are present in that tissue - and in what quantities . The latter is something known as \" expression \" . Cancer is a disease where part of your body has decided to mutate in some way . Instead of growing and acting normally , the cells in your body have decided to grow unrestrained and start to take over the normal , healthy cells . Cancer is not like a cold or polio where there is a single virus or bacteria that caused it . It 's caused by your body mutating itself . So the big question is - why ? Why did the cells in your body decide to start mutating ? That 's what we use microarrays to help figure out . By putting a tumor sample on a microarray , we measure what genes are present in the tumor and in what quantities . The hope is that we can compare there results of the tumor measurement to that from a sample of normal tissue and determine which genes are possibly contributing to the mutation . For example , we might see that in a lung cancer patient that gene XXXX should have a level of 2 , but in their tumor has a level of 10 . What this means is that the DNA in the cells in the tumor are expressing too much of gene XXXX . Our next step is to then suppress gene XXXX , typically by finding a drug that is designed to do so . In fact , this can be a drug designed for any purpose at all - as long as it suppresses gene XXXX , we think it can treat this cancer . This idea of picking a drug to target a gene that we identify as \" out of spec \" is the core of what we call \" personalized medicine \" and is , IMO , a large part of the future of cancer treatment and healthcare is general . So what , exactly , is a microarray ? Well , the ones I 've seen in person are about the size of an ID badge . In the middle is the array , which is an N x M grid of little divots , each containing what we call a \" probe \" . It 's not a probe in the \" anal \" sense of extra - terrestrials , but really just a tiny bit of organic molecules . Each divot contains molecules that react with specific genes in a predictable way - older microarry probes actually changed color . So you put a tissue sample on the array , each probe reacts to the genes ( RNA actually ) in the tissue , and a computer reads the resulting color of the probe - much like you would look at the color of litmus paper to tell if something is an acid or a base .", "Let me try and see if i can eli5 this :P So you know certain genes are responsible for a disease , you sequence them and then create probes for them . These are just the opposite sequence ( so if u have a ATTGCC gene sequence , the probe would have a TAACGG ) . When a sequence matches the opposite of probe , they will have the tendency to hybridize ( AKA bind together ) . The microarray chip is composed of little wells , where each of the well contains a different probe for different genes . This allows for testing for lots of genes with each essay . So now you take your sample RNA ( so you know if the DNA is being read by the cell ) , make a bunch of copies into DNA and mark them with a dye . You slap those in the micro array chip and see which well have the dye present . Since you know which gene corresponds to which well you now know which genes are being expressed . There 's some variations of this , but this is the basis of microarrays . Btw there 's a new fad , the next generation sequencing techniques that might take over microarrays as soon as they 're cheaper and more accurate . You might wanna check those out . Source : I have a biochem major and am a bioinformatics master student"]
chatgpt_answers: ["DNA microarrays are lab tools used to examine DNA samples. Think of them as tiny chips or glass slides covered with a grid of minuscule spots, and each spot holds a particular DNA sequence. First, scientists prepare DNA from a person or another organism. question: How do apps like shazam and soundhound work ? Once they listen to the song , how do they find out what song it is ? Do they find like a matching audio file or something like that ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["I do n't know about Soundhound , but for Shazam specifically , here is how it works ( or at least did a few years ago when I read about this ) ; As the Shazam app records the song , it will split it up into short blocks of time . Within each period of time , it will figure out what frequency is the loudest . Assuming that the music is the loudest thing happening , this does an excellent job of filtering out any background noise and quiet notes that may be imperceptible to a crappy cell phone microphone . It does n't matter if people are talking or a door opens or if your cell mic ca n't pick out that exquisite bass guitar in the background -- only the loudest thing matters . The result is a \" fingerprint \" of the song --- a list of the loudest frequencies versus time for a short fragment . The frequencies are rounded off here , because 14224 kHz is not going to be distinguishable from 14000 kHz for the purposes of Shazam and your crappy cell mic . Because you obviously do n't record the entire song , the actual fingerprint you get on your phone will be a subset of the complete fingerprint of the song . Like this ( random letters used to represent frequencies ) Full song : ABAJUKARWA Clip : UKA ( Note that , at this point , Shazam does n't know that the clip and the full song are associated , much less where in the full song the clip appears , but they are presented together for demonstration ) . Note that the fingerprint from your mic is actually pretty close to the real one , even though you just held your crappy cell phone towards the mall speaker for ten seconds . That is for a few reasons : 1 . As previously mentioned , only the loudest sounds are used 2 . Because you 're not listening to radio static , the song at 59.2 seconds sounds really similar to the song at 59.3 seconds . Therefore the impact of your time intervals not necessarily being aligned with the \" official \" time intervals is reduced . Now , Shazam has invested a lot of money into buying studio music and fingerprinting entire albums . So they have an enormous database of songs and their associated complete fingerprints ( what the loudest sounds are in each small time fragment for the entire song ) . Your phone uploads the fingerprint it took to the Shazam server . Shazam uses a proprietary fuzzy matching algorithm to very quickly search their huge database of fingerprints for portions that are very close to your fingerprint . They do n't use an exact match ; they actually use a line similarity algorithm . Basically , imagine the full song as an XY graph with time as X and loudest frequency in that time fragment as Y. Your clip is a small part of that graph . Under studio conditions the lines would be exactly identical . On a crappy cell mic , they wo n't be , but : 1 . They will be quite close ( if the song 's loudest frequency is 20kHz at some point , your mic might might be a frequency band or so up or down , but it 's not going to be 7kHz ) 2 . They will have the same patterns of rise and fall , 3 . If there are deviations due to loud noises , they will be short ( maybe someone talked or a door opened , but that 's just a big blip and then it goes back to normal ) . Using these three facts , Shazam is able to do a really , really good job of identifying what song your clip came from , and where . And then it sends back the result ! :)", "It 's like a fingerprint . Shazam has a huge library of songs for which it has already analyzed the frequencies and created a \" digital fingerprint , \" like a [ spectrogram ] ( URL_0 ) . When you use it to identify a song , it performs this same analysis on the music it 's \" listening \" to and compares the results to its library to find the song that matches . Like a human fingerprint , a particular song will have a unique pattern of frequencies , which is why it usually only needs to \" hear \" a small clip in order to narrow it down enough find the matching song .", "Most likely it analyzes the piece of sound waves you make it listen to , and compares that real time with a database full of songs and sound waves . I 'm no expert in this field , but there are tons of algorithms designed to understand music better , so I 'm sure Shazam and Soundhound got a hold of those algorithms ."]

Here’s the basic idea: when you open the app and tap the “listen” button, it records the sound coming through your microphone. After that, it turns that sound into a digital “fingerprint,” which is a small set of data that represents the song’s audio traits. That fingerprint gets sent to a server, and the server checks it against a database of songs to see whether anything matches. If it finds one, it sends the song info back to the app, and the app shows you the title and artist.

So it’s not really hunting for a matching audio file. It’s comparing the unique fingerprint of the sound it hears with fingerprints stored in its database to identify the song."]

question: What is the difference between computer hardware and smartphone hardware ? Extended question : What is preventing people from developing proper drivers and running iOS Android devices and vise versa like you can with osx and windows ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["The main difference is that most smartphones have ARM processors , whereas PC 's have either Intel or AMD . More specifically , smartphones use a RISC architecture , whereas PC 's use CISC . RISC stands for \" reduced instruction set computing . \" What that means is when an instruction ( command ) gets passed to the processor , it does exactly one thing . So , if I wanted to add two numbers together , the assembly code might look something like : lw $ t0 , 0($s0 ) //load the first number from memory lw $ t1 , 4($s0 ) //load the second number from memory add $ t2 , $ t0 , $ t1 //add the two numbers sw $ t3 , 8($s0 ) //store the result in memory In other words , I 'm doing four steps to perform one operation ( addition ) . In a CISC ( complex instruction set computing ) architecture , instructions can do more than one task at a time . I do n't know the actual assembly for it , but I could write the stuff up there in one line in CISC . The key difference is that the CISC instruction can do the load and store commands in the same instruction as the addition . The advantage of CISC is that you can perform more advanced tasks with fewer instructions , but the disadvantage is that the increased level of complexity means you need more power and resources . RISC is \" lean \" and efficient , but not quite as flexible . None of this necessarily has anything to do with why you ca n't put iOS on your phone . If I had to hazard a guess , I would say that it could be done , but nobody has enough motivation ( financial or otherwise ) to actually do it .", "People can and do . I used to run Android on my iPhone 3 G , but the drivers were not so good , especially for power management , which meant that it would empty a full battery in about 40 minutes . This is a licensing and marketing issue , not a technical one . Originally , PCs had to make it very easy for people to load whatever OS they wanted , because there was no clearly dominant OS and people who were into early computers were enthusiasts who liked to tinker . Today , Apple has a vested interest in convincing people to switch from Windows , so they go out of their way to make it possible for people to load both OSs on their hardware , so that people can get a migration path - a way to try out Mac OS without fear of losing Windows forever . The PC architecture still descends from the original PC , which made it easy to load different OSs . Microsoft is working hard to change this , but for now they need to keep compatible , and also there are many niche applications that rely on OSs other than Windows in the PC arena , such as Linux ( and other Unix variants ) on servers , specialized computers for scientific instruments , and more . On the smartphone side , on the other hand , Apple started by itself . They have no need and no incentive to be compatible with anything else , and they try to protect their market by making it as hard as possible to install anything other than iOS on an iPhone . More or less the same goes for the other players , who came later to this market . There is some basic computer science that shows that they can make it difficult , but never impossible , to run any software on any hardware . Finally , there 's a matter of licensing and legality . Just because it 's technically possible , it does n't mean it 's legal .", "Size basically . Also the stuff in smartphones tends to be optimized to use less power and generate less heat . question: Why is it that calling / texting another country costs more but when visiting a website that s hosted in another country does n't effect price . Surely they use similar cable runs ( not share ) so where s the cost incurred ? Explain like I'm five.

The telephone system is separate from the internet , and uses different cables . The big difference is that while the internet uses packages , the phone system uses a dedicated channel , which gets set up when you place the call , and stays in place while the call is active . The cable between the different countries can only handle a certain number of channels , so they charge for usage of them , to prevent them from getting flooded . VOIP works for the most part , but has to deal with bigger lag ( as each package gets routed separately ) , dropped packages , and the possibility of packages arriving out of order ( if they took different routes ) . The dedicated channel for telephones means that once you are connected , you generally stay connected .", "It 's a thing from the past , but companies are still milking it . Essentially , back in the day phone lines where actual copper all the way . Copper run from your house to your local exchange , and from there nationwide . International calls where part copper , part radio , radio being either land - to - land or land - satellite - land . The entire network did n't belong to the same operator , and even if it did , he still had increased costs for a longer call . Satellite bandwidth is insanely expensive . Nowdays , it 's all IP . Even companies that sell you regular landlines have their backbones in IP , and their connection to other carriers is over IP too . It 's fiber all the way , and a phone call uses relatively low bandwidth , so the physical cost of actually calling someone on the other side of the globe is negligible , most of their cost is in the last mile and backbone . But of course , companies are still milking a thing of the past , so , when you want to buy minutes from another exchange , that exchange will sell you minutes at a different price than it sells them locally , even if that provider is the local exchange for that particular destination . So , say , the phone company in Somewhere , Some country will sell minutes at 0.01 to their local users in Somewhere , or will even offer unlimited plans , but when Someotherplace , Someothercountry wants to interconnect with them , Somewhere will sell ( and buy ) those same minutes at a higher price , when actually their cost of selling them to Someotherplace is lower , since it 's a telco to telco interconnection , which most of the time is just a SIP account , and the maintenance and setup costs of that trunk is lower than a single last mile for a single local customer ."]

So a middle ground is found and we are adapted to have babies born relatively undeveloped , so their skull bone plates are not fused yet and their brain is smaller . So we have covered why childbirth is painful and babies are annoying little shits .", "Because natural selection is a process , not a choice . The only way that painful childbirth would be removed by natural selection is if all women who had painful labors died . That would leave women who had pain - free childbirth to continue having babies , therefore carrying on the genetic predisposition to pain - free labor . Natural selection works by removing the least effective method of survival . Painful childbirth does n't limit survival . Besides all of that , women forget the pain of childbirth fairly quickly . I 've had 3 children naturally , with no drugs . I 'm certain that it hurt at the time . I have memory of being in pain , but I do n't actually remember what the pain felt like . Go back a few million years , or look at basically any other category of mammals ( the canines , felines , ruminants , etc . ) and you 'll find way smaller heads relative to the size of the birth canal . But somewhere along the line , our branch of the tree of life stumbled upon the fact that bigger brains turn out to be a survival advantage , and we 've pushed that trait for thousands upon thousands of generations , until we 've ended up with the huge freakin ' melons we 've got today . Yes , this puts stress on the rest of the female reproductive system , which has had to evolve to keep up , which is why humans have such large openings in our pelvises . It 's all about those damn baby heads . But again , evolution does n't give a shit if it hurts . It only matters if the baby survives , and the mother survives to take care of it . question: How are engineers able to build subway tunnels under already built infrastructure ? It amazes me when I 'm in the subway in big cities how these tunnels have been dug beneath skyscapers ? How do the builders do it ? Please explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["There are multiple ways that they can do it . Here in Boston where I live they finished the [ Big Dig ] ( URL_2 ) a few years ago . Over the course of roughly ten years they consolidated all the utilities through a corridor of the city and then sank an entire elevated highway underground . It had to go above and below various obstacles like a subway tunnel , a waterway , and Boston Harbor itself . The main corridor of the sunken highway , I-93 , was built in multiple sections . In parts of it they simply closed down the ground - level street for a few months , dug a 100 foot deep pit , built the tunnel , then filled the hole back in . In another part where they had to dig directly under the existing elevated highway they first sunk steel girders 100 feet down to the bedrock , filled the area around them with concrete , then supported the existing highway on top of that while digging out the dirt and building the tunnel underneath . At one spot they had to build the tunnel underneath an existing subway station that was itself underneath a very busy surface road in the middle of downtown Boston . To do this they first dug two vertical shafts to below the level of the subway station . They then dug horizontal tunnels underneath the station , supporting them with concrete . From those tunnels they dug hundreds of tiny shafts and filled them up with concrete . Eventually they formed it all into a complete set of concrete walls that formed the new tunnel . If you scroll close to [ the bottom of this page ] ( URL_1 ) you can see a series of graphics that show how it was done . Another section of tunnel had to be built under multiple train tracks just outside one of Boston 's main train stations where thousands of commuters pass every day . The soil under the train tracks was pretty soft so they had to figure out a way of building this tunnel without making the tracks sink into a sinkhole or anything like that . What they did in this case was to sink hundreds of pipes into the ground and then injected freezing water into them in order to freeze the ground solid . They then dug a pit on one side of the tracks and built an entire tunnel section there . They then used giant hydraulic jacks to pull the tunnel section underneath the tracks as they slowly dug out the frozen dirt underneath them . [ Here 's a page ] ( URL_0 ) that describes that process . Although not used in Boston , another typical way of building tunnels is by using a tunnel boring machine ( TBM ) . A TBM is basically a crawling machine that can dig out dirt or rock in front of it and then immediately seal the circular hole it creates with reinforced concrete .", "Very , very carefully . They 'd normally avoid it , and subway tunnels go under roads : but if they dig deep enough to avoid weakening the foundations of a building , it can be done , assuming the local geology will allow . Sometimes buildings may be built with the expectation that a subway tunnel will be built under it in the near future , and the foundations designed accordingly . It does n't always go well , though . In 2009 , construction of a subway line near the Historcal Archive of the City of Cologne , Germany , caused the ancient building and two other buildings to collapse , mercifully causing only two deaths . However , 90 % of the archive , including documents going back to the year 922 , was buried . About 85 % of them have been recovered , but some of them badly damaged . A new building is due for completion in 2017 , but restoring the documents is likely to take another 30 years . The tunnel was n't even under the building , but next to it , and being built using the traditional \" cut and cover \" method . But water seeped out of the tunnel and into the buildings ' foundations ; workers were able to raise the alarm in time , hence the very few casualties , but only just .", "With London if I remember correctly there had n't been good documentation of buildings to account for depth of all that the tube would pass under . Some lines like the Piccadilly line and Northern line are incredible deep to account for this . Others building surveryors were called in to assess already built buildings to judge whether they could withstand a tube line being built and at what depth ."]

TBMs can also install the tracks and electrical wiring while moving forward, so once the tunnel is complete, the subway can begin operating right away."]

question: Why does alcohol kill bacteria when yeast -a bactera- produces alcohol ? Would n't the yeast all die as they produce alcohol ? Please explain like I'm five.

It 's a ' unicellular eukaryote ' which is science lingo for : it 's organized on a cellular level like most of the big stuff on the planet , but it lives alone . We are eukaryotes , and on a cellular level are extremely similar to yeast . Where we start getting different is in the next level of structure : Our cells clump together and start working together to produce tissues , which make up organs , which make whole animals ; Yeast do n't do that . Every yeast organism will live and die all by it 's self . It never teams up with its friends to build a bigger ' something ' . Just a minor thing , but kind of an obvious error in your question . Before I can get to the subject of your question , I need to cover some background info . All living organisms are comprised of cells . Cells are rather complicated , but fundamentally are a drop of water(with alot of stuff dissolved in it ) , shielded from the world inside an oil droplet . This oil makes up the ' cell membrane ' and it gives the cell a distinct boundary , and helps the cell hold onto ' what it owns ' or what 's inside that oil wall . Cells need to ' hold onto ' a certain amount of nutrients , and various molecules otherwise they die ; if they lose their cell membrane , they die . That extremely thin wall of oil is of the utmost importance to a cell 's survival . Alcohol , kills things by dissolving their cell membrane , and releasing everything that used to be inside the cell . Normally Oil and water do n't mix , but alcohols serve as middle men , and allows them to get along just fine . There is some cool chemistry involved in that , but it 's a bit past ELI5 I think . Now , a little of alcohol in water is n't going to spontaneously dissolve all the oil in it , or kill any cells it runs into ; you need to get to a higher concentration before this starts happening . That 's why you normally find 75 - 95 % ethanol / isopropyl alcohol as a cleaning agent against bacteria , it 's complete overkill and that 's what we like when cleaning . Yeast does n't normally make ethanol actually , it does that as a back up plan . Left alone under normal conditions , yeast actually likes to make carbon dioxide using oxygen . Ethanol is a biproduct of fermentation , which only happens when yeast is oxygen starved , like in a fermentation vat , or a wine bottle . Lets go with the wine bottle for a bit as an example . You throw some yeast in grape juice , and seal it up . The yeast starts using what oxygen is around in order to produce energy so it can survive , and as a byproduct produces Co2 . This keeps going until the oxygen is depleted . Now , at this point the yeast has two options : produce no energy and Die , or start generating energy in a way that does n't require oxygen . Yeast decide to do the second , but this is a ' hail mary play ' as it were because the byproduct of this avenue is ethanol , which once enough of it is produced , will kill the yeast as discussed above . It 's a gamble that oxygen will come back before the yeast makes enough ethanol to kill itself . In wine making , and industry processes , the yeast always dies because we care about the ethanol , not the yeast . Poor yeast ... but on the bright side , you can get up to 20 % ethanol by it 's noble sacrifice ! Jesus allegedly died for your sins ... Millions of Yeast definitely died for that drink . Stay Thirsty my Friends .", "Yeast is a fungi not a bacteria .", "Why does yeast produce alcohol ? To kill off other microorganisms that might compete with it for food . question: Why do software sometimes crashes ? Why a piece of software stop working ? Is it because it 's trying to divide per zero , for example ? Why do not programmers fully test software before selling it to you ? Please explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Programmer here . There are * lots * of reasons why a piece of software may crash . A divide - by - zero error is one possible reason , though not the most common . Running out of memory -- or accessing the wrong section of memory -- is another potential cause . Another possibility is running out of the bounds of an array -- for example , if the program 's code says to \" look up the first five items \" , and there are only four items . The number of potential reasons for a program to crash are as numerous as programs themselves . Why is n't the software fully tested ? Well ... we try , as a rule . But the number of use - cases ( i.e. , \" What can the user do ? \" ) for complex software can make this difficult , especially if the business department is pushing for the software to be released soon ( buggy software can be patched , and unreleased software earns no money . ) To look at a relatively simple program , consider a calculator . Adding two positive numbers together is a use case . Adding a negative and a positive is a different use case . So is adding an integer to a non - integer . So is multiplying two positive integers . Every possible combination of operators and types of numbers is a different use case . And that 's just a calculator . After a certain point , it becomes difficult to even remember all the possible use cases , let alone test for all of them . Most programmers I know will try to test as they go along , but even then , and even with separate QA departments ( which you 'll find at most larger companies ) , it 's possible for certain things to slip through . And then there are the problems that arise from outside one 's own code . Software does n't exist in a vacuum . If I write a program , it has to run on your machine , with that machine 's hardware and operating system , and the drivers that are installed on it .... And all of * those * things have their own bugs . Even if the code I wrote were theoretically perfect , it could crash due to something being wrong with one of those . If my company has a large QA department , they can cover a lot of different combinations of OSs , drivers , hardware , and so forth ... but they can never cover every combination , at least not with computers ( consoles , having a uniform configuration , were a different matter , though nowadays that 's changed as well due to being able to upgrade system software . ) If you 've ever noticed with some games that some people have a consistent crash problem and other players do n't , this is often the reason why : it 's a bug that comes about through the interaction with a specific combination of hardware and software , and not all users have that combination . And finally , there are cases that are simple user error . Sometimes this can be accounted for by the programmer ; I can prevent you from entering the word \" blue \" when I 'm asking for a number . But a programmer ca n't prevent you from , for example , running more programs than your system can handle . If someone attempted to run three separate 3D renderers on a 4 GB memory system and then started up * Batman : Arkham City * , it would n't matter how good a job the programmers did at making all those programs operate efficiently , they 're going to bring that system to a screeching halt .", "The reasons for program crashes are vast , in practice there are several that cover almost all cases : Bad pointer : Trying to dereference a piece of memory that has not been allocated , or previously allocated but later deallocated with your program not acknowledging it . Unhandled Exception : Your divide by zero is one example of an exception in IEEE 754 . An exception is not strictly an unrecoverable error , but rather an extreme condition that could potentially be reasonable handled by default but is often better yielded to the program . If such an exception is thrown and the program does not have a handling routine for it the program halts due to lacking a determine execution path . Unresponsive : APIs such as Windows and DirectX expect a program to regularly call on these , such as to retrieve new input data . If a program has a long running process in the same thread as these contexts are created without regularly pausing to acknowledge the APIs , the program will fail to update inputs , window , etc . The program might be able to recover once this long running process is finished , but quite often the OS marks it as unresponsive , as does the user . Such continuous processes should either be chunked ( not run for longer than milliseconds at a time ) , or ran in a separate thread . Buffer overflow : For example allocating 4 bytes for some user input ( name ) [ M][A][R][K ] , having the user input a longer name and attempting to write this to memory without checking length [ F][R][A][N ] K. This is related to bad pointers in the sense that an attempt is being made to read or write a piece of memory that the program has not requested beforehand . This does n't necessarily lead to conventional overflow crashes however . If the memory being overflowed to has been allocated you will be stomping on some other piece of your data without the OS being aware . This could result in all sorts of bizarre behaviors . Stack overflow : When a function executes it creates a local context in some preallocated piece of memory . When one function calls another and that one calls another without the prior function terminating ( this is often seen in recursive algorithms ) , if this tree grows sufficiently large it will no longer be able to fit into the stack . Logical error : In this case the program is n't distinctly misbehaving with respect to the OS or memory , however it contains some flawed piece of logical that leads to crash - like effects . The program might for one reason or another decide that window updates are no longer necessary effectively freezing the program visually .", "> Why do not programmers fully test software before selling it to you ? Because it 's effectively impossible . One huge problem is that you are developing for a system that is n't testable . So , I might make a program on my computer but there is something in your configuration that messes things up ."]
chatgpt_answers: ["Software can stop working, or crash, for lots of different reasons. Sometimes it happens because the program is asked to do something impossible, like divide by zero, which is a math operation that isn’t allowed. Other times, it may run out of memory or use too much processing power, or a bug in the code may make it act in a way the programmer didn’t expect. Programmers do try hard to test software before it’s sold, but they can’t always catch every problem. Software can be extremely complicated, and it’s hard to know exactly how it will behave in every possible situation. On top of that, people often use software in ways the creators never planned for, and that can cause new issues. Even with all of that, programmers still work hard to make their software as dependable and stable as they can."]

question: Russell 's Paradox This is a topic that we were discussing today briefly in math . I 'm having trouble visualizing it . Can someone help me out ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Russell 's paradox is : Let R be the set of all sets that do not contain themselves . Is R in R ? If R is in R , then R contains itself , so by the definition of R , R is not in R. If R is not in R , then R does not contain itself , so by the definition of R , R is in R. As an analogy , let 's think about a town full of people instead of sets . R is going to be a barber . And instead of talking about containing , we 'll talk about shaving . Russell 's paradox becomes : There 's a town with a barber . In this town , the barber shaves everyone who does not shave themselves . Who shaves the barber ? If the barber does not shave himself , then the barber shaves himself ( because the barber shaves * everyone * who does not shave themselves ) . If the barber does shave himself , then the barber does not shave himself ( because the barber does n't shave anyone who does shave themselves ) .", "It 's a way to say with sets , \" This statement is a lie . \"", "Suppose you 're going through your computer madly organizing files into folders and folders into other folders into other folders and so on . You 're creating the ultimate organization system . You have a \" movies \" folder and then a \" folders which contain folders which contain movies \" folder , which of course contains the \" movies \" folder , and a \" folder which contains folders which contain folders which contain entertainment \" foder , which of course contains the \" folders which contain folders which contain movies \" folder . And so on . You 're crazy . So at one point it occurs to you that your computer is letting you put folders * inside of themselves * . That is , you 're able to store the \" movies \" folder in the \" movies \" folder . You open up the movies folder and it says like - Spice World ( 1997 HD rip ) - Porn Folder - Movies Folder And if you click \" Movies Folder \" , it opens up the exact same folder . So you decide , in order to be truly organized , you ought to make a folder called the \" Folders which contain themselves \" folder . So , Movies Folder goes in there , since you just put Movies Folder in Movies Folder a moment ago . But \" Documents \" , which only contains a short story you started working on a little while ago , does not go in \" Folders which contain themselves \" . Now for the sake of completeness , you create another folder called \" Folders which do not contain themselves \" . You think that this way you can just have these two folders on your desktop — since * every * folder ought to fit in one of those , right ? After all , a folder either does or does not contain itself . If it does it goes in the first folder , and if it does n't it goes in the second folder . But I ask you : does \" Folders which do not contain themselves \" contain itself ? Well suppose it does . Then it is a folder which contains itself , so it certainly does not belong in \" Folders which do not contain themselves \" . So it must not contain itself , right ? Well if it does n't , then it is a folder which does not contain itself , so it * does * belong in \" Folders which do not contain themselves \" . So it does if it does n't and it does n't if it does . That 's a problem ."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Of course! Russell’s paradox is what happens when we try to make “the set of all sets that do not contain themselves.”

Think of a set A that includes every set that is not a member of itself. So if some set B belongs to A, that means B does not contain itself.

Now ask about a set like B: does it contain itself or not?

- If B does contain itself, then it cannot belong to A, because A only has sets that do not contain themselves. But if B is not in A, then B should be one of the sets that do not contain themselves, which means it should be in A. That’s a contradiction.
- If B does not contain itself, then it fits the rule for A and should be in A. But if it is in A, then it must not contain itself. That also leads to a contradiction.

So either way, we get stuck. That impossible loop is the paradox.

Bertrand Russell found this in 1901, and it shook up math a lot because it made people question the basic foundations of set theory and mathematics."]

question: How can something sound like it 's going up in pitch , but really it 's not ? ( Example song inside ) From the best game ever : URL_0 But really , how does that work ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["That is referred to as a [ Shepard Scale ] ( URL_0 ) . Essentially , several scales are played at once with the pitch of all of them rising in sync , but with the volume of each fading away at the high pitches , and a new \" voice \" fading in at the low pitches .", "This video by Vi Hart does an awesome job explaining some of the principles behind pitches : URL_0 What I think they 're doing in that music is they 're playing with overtones . Vi Hart demonstrates an interesting trick . If you hear ( say ) sound at 440 Hz , 660 Hz , 880 Hz , and 1100 Hz , it sounds like it 's at 220 , because your brain knows that a 220 Hz pitch is the most likely explanation for why you 're hearing all those overtones . So here 's an aural illusion for you . Suppose I play tones at 220 , 440 , 660 , 880 , 1100 , and 1320 at volumes 0 , 10 , 8 , 6 , 4 , and 2 , respectively . At this moment the 220 tone is totally muted , but it sounds like the pitch is at 220 . Now suppose over the next 5 seconds , I smoothly double each frequency , and smoothly diminish the volumes from 10 to 8 , from 8 to 6 , etc , but smoothly increase the volume of the lowest tone from 0 to 10 . From moment to moment , it sounds like the sound is going up : about 1/3 of a second in , the tones would be 230 , 460 , 690 , 920 , 1150 , and 1380 , which will sound like a 230 Hz pitch , a little higher than the 220 we started with . But after the full 5 seconds , we 're exactly back where we started . The 220 at 0 volume has become a 440 at volume 10 , the 440 at volume 10 has become 660 at volume 8 , and so on . I think this is what 's happening in that song , but they 're covering it up by having the music move quickly .", "The official name for this phenomenon / technique is The Shepard Tone . When you know what you 're listening to , you can hear it ' start over ' . For example , while watching the video you linked to , I could hear the bass come back in , and shortly after , the higher notes faded away . Seeing as though the scale is never actually ' resolved ' , our brains are always waiting for the end , and when the notes in question get closer to that end , they become quieter . By the time our brain can even start to notice this , other , louder and lower notes have taken their place . That 's a weird and not very scientific explanation , but I hope it helps . URL_0"]
chatgpt_answers:
["In the song you linked, it seems like the pitch is rising because the notes are arranged in an upward-moving pattern. But the pitch of each note isn’t actually changing. What’s changing is the sequence, and that sequence makes your brain hear it as going higher.

Think of it like climbing stairs. Say there are ten steps, and each step is a different pitch. If you walk from the bottom step to the top, it feels like you’re going higher and higher. Still, each step stays exactly where it is — you’re just moving through them in order. That’s what’s happening in music here.

So in music, notes can act like stairs: when they’re played in a pattern that keeps moving upward, your ear hears a rise in pitch even if the notes themselves aren’t sliding upward."]

They search the World Wide Web in a systematic way for particular information specified in a textual web search query. The search results are generally presented in a line of results, often referred to as search engine results pages (SERPs). When a user enters a query into a search engine, the engine scans its index of web pages to find those that are relevant to the user's query. The results are then ranked by relevancy and displayed to the user. The information may be a mix of links to web pages, images, videos, infographics, articles, research papers, and other types of files. Some search engines also mine data available in databases or open directories. Unlike web directories and social bookmarking sites, which are maintained by human editors, search engines also maintain real-time information by running an algorithm on a web crawler. Any internet-based content that can't be indexed and searched by a web search engine falls under the category of deep web."]
chatgpt_answers: ["Web search is the process of looking up information on the World Wide Web, or the internet. To do a web search, you type a query—a word or phrase describing what you want to find—into a search engine. The search engine then scans its index of websites and produces a set of results that relate to your query. Common search engines include Google, Bing, and Yahoo. Each one relies on its own ranking algorithms to decide which pages are most relevant. You can usually use a search engine by visiting its site directly or by entering a search term in the search bar built into your web browser. Web search is helpful for locating information on many subjects, such as news, images, videos, products, and more. It can also help you discover websites you might not have found otherwise."]

question: Please explain what is "Google Search"

question: Please explain what is "Recommender system"
human_answers: ["A recommender system, or a recommendation system (sometimes replacing 'system' with a synonym such as platform or engine), is a subclass of information filtering system that provide suggestions for items that are most pertinent to a particular user. Typically, the suggestions refer to various decision-making processes, such as what product to purchase, what music to listen to, or what online news to read. Recommender systems are particularly useful when an individual needs to choose an item from a potentially overwhelming number of items that a service may offer.\nRecommender systems are used in a variety of areas, with commonly recognised examples taking the form of playlist generators for video and music services, product recommenders for online stores, or content recommenders for social media platforms and open web content recommenders. These systems can operate using a single input, like music, or multiple inputs within and across platforms like news, books and search queries. There are also popular recommender systems for specific topics like restaurants and online dating. Recommender systems have also been developed to explore research articles and experts, collaborators, and financial services."]
chatgpt_answers:
["A recommender system, also called a recommendation system, is a type of information filtering system designed to estimate the “rating” or preference a user would assign to an item. These systems are used across many fields, but they are best known as playlist creators in music and video services, product suggestion engines in online shopping, or content recommendation tools on social media platforms.  

Recommender systems can be built in several ways, including:  

Collaborative filtering: This approach generates suggestions from the past behavior of a user group. It examines what similar users have liked before and recommends those same items to a new user.  

Content-based filtering: This approach bases recommendations on item attributes. It analyzes an item’s features and then suggests similar items to the user.  

Hybrid systems: These combine collaborative filtering and content-based filtering when producing recommendations.  

Common uses include product suggestions on e-commerce sites, movie recommendations for streaming platforms, and content recommendations on social media. By tailoring suggestions and drawing attention to items a user may find especially relevant, recommender systems can improve the overall user experience."]

question: Please explain what is "Automated reasoning"
human_answers:
["In computer science, in particular in knowledge representation and reasoning and metalogic, the area of automated reasoning is dedicated to understanding different aspects of reasoning. The study of automated reasoning helps produce computer programs that allow computers to reason completely, or nearly completely, automatically. Although automated reasoning is considered a sub-field of artificial intelligence, it also has connections with theoretical computer science and philosophy.
The most developed subareas of automated reasoning are automated theorem proving (and the less automated but more pragmatic subfield of interactive theorem proving) and automated proof checking (viewed as guaranteed correct reasoning under fixed assumptions).[citation needed] Extensive work has also been done in reasoning by analogy using induction and abduction.
Other important topics include reasoning under uncertainty and non-monotonic reasoning. An important part of the uncertainty field is that of argumentation, where further constraints of minimality and consistency are applied on top of the more standard automated deduction. John Pollock's OSCAR system is an example of an automated argumentation system that is more specific than being just an automated theorem prover.
Tools and techniques of automated reasoning include the classical logics and calculi, fuzzy logic, Bayesian inference, reasoning with maximal entropy and many less formal ad hoc techniques."]
chatgpt_answers: ["Automated reasoning refers to the use of computer programs to derive new conclusions automatically from a collection of statements or axioms. As a branch of artificial intelligence and computer science, it focuses on building algorithms and systems that can carry out logical reasoning and reach decisions through deductions from known facts or axioms. Its uses are broad, spanning theorem proving, diagnosis, planning, and natural language processing. It appears in areas such as mathematics, computer science, engineering, and artificial intelligence, where it helps with tasks that would be too difficult or impractical for people to do by hand.  \n\nThere are multiple approaches to automated reasoning, including rule-based systems, resolution-based systems, model-based systems, and hybrid systems that combine several methods. These systems may rely on different logical formalisms, such as propositional logic, first-order logic, and higher-order logics, to represent knowledge and reason about it. In short, automated reasoning is about having a computer reason logically and make decisions from known facts or axioms. It is a powerful way to tackle complex problems and carry out work that would be infeasible for humans to perform manually."]

question: Please explain what is "Information theory"
human_answers: ["Information theory is the scientific study of the quantification, storage, and communication of information. The field was originally established by the works of Harry Nyquist and Ralph Hartley, in the 1920s, and Claude Shannon in the 1940s.: vii  The field is at the intersection of probability theory, statistics, computer science, statistical mechanics, information engineering, and electrical engineering.\nA key measure in information theory is entropy. Entropy quantifies the amount of uncertainty involved in the value of a random variable or the outcome of a random process. For example, identifying the outcome of a fair coin flip (with two equally likely outcomes) provides less information (lower entropy) than specifying the outcome from a roll of a die (with six equally likely outcomes). Some other important measures in information theory are mutual information, channel capacity, error exponents, and relative entropy. Important sub-fields of information theory include source coding, algorithmic complexity theory, algorithmic information theory and information-theoretic security.\nApplications of fundamental topics of information theory include source coding/data compression (e.g. for ZIP files), and channel coding/error detection and correction (e.g. for DSL). Its impact has been crucial to the success of the Voyager missions to deep space, the invention of the compact disc, the feasibility of mobile phones and the development of the Internet. The theory has also found applications in other areas, including statistical inference, cryptography, neurobiology, perception, linguistics, the evolution and function of molecular codes (bioinformatics), thermal physics, molecular dynamics, quantum computing, black holes, information retrieval, intelligence gathering, plagiarism detection,[10] pattern recognition, anomaly detection[11] and even art creation."]
chatgpt_answers: ["Information theory is a field of mathematics concerned with how information is processed, sent, and stored. Claude Shannon developed it in the 1940s to make the idea of information precise and to measure how much information can pass through a given channel. Its core insight is that information can be expressed in terms of uncertainty: the less certain an event is, the more information its outcome carries. For instance, if you already know a coin is fair, a flip is equally likely to come up heads or tails, so the result gives you little information. But if you are unsure whether the coin is fair, the outcome is less predictable, and the information gained is greater. Information theory uses entropy to measure uncertainty or randomness in a system; greater uncertainty means higher entropy. It also defines mutual information, which measures how much one random variable tells you about another. The field is widely used in computer science, engineering, and statistics, where it helps in building efficient communication systems, compressing data, analyzing statistical data, and understanding the limits of computation."]
